title: Bipartisan Great American AI Act draft proposes three-year state-law preemption
subtitle: The 269-page House discussion draft from Reps. Obernolte and Trahan would pause state AI development laws while standing up a federal framework for frontier developers.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: On June 4, 2026, Reps. Jay Obernolte (R-Calif.) and Lori Trahan (D-Mass.) released a 269-page discussion draft titled the Great American Artificial Intelligence Act. The bill bundles a three-year preemption of state laws that regulate AI model development, mandatory Frontier AI Frameworks for companies with $500 million or more in revenue, required incident reporting for critical safety events, $100 million a year for a federal AI standards center, and criminal penalties for AI-assisted impersonation of government officials. Four co-sponsors signed on at release: Reps. Subramanyam, Franklin, Peters, and Houchin — an even three-three partisan split.

The preemption clause is the part everyone is fighting over. California's AB 2013, which requires model developers to publish training-data summaries, would be paused for three years. Part of SB 942, which covers content watermarking, would also be paused. Preemption only covers development — laws about how AI gets used or deployed stay in place at the state level. Brad Carson, president of Americans for Responsible Innovation, called the cap a generational mistake, arguing it turns the current state-law floor into a federal ceiling. Industry trade groups generally welcomed the draft as relief from a patchwork they say is unworkable to comply with.

This is the first serious bipartisan AI bill to clear discussion-draft stage in this Congress. It lands while the Trump administration's June 2 executive order on voluntary frontier-model testing is still being interpreted, and while the EU AI Act's transparency rules are queued to take effect in August. The U.S. has spent two years watching individual states — California, Colorado, New York — write AI rules in parallel. The Obernolte-Trahan draft is the first credible attempt to force the conversation back to the federal level. Whether it survives committee in anything close to its current form is the open question.

A note for learners: if you build with AI, the regulatory layer you'll comply with in 2028 will probably look more like this draft than like the current state-by-state mess. Read the actual text now — even at 269 pages — because the early discussion-draft phase is when industry input has the most leverage. By the time the bill is on the House floor, the architecture is locked.
